# The impact of digital literacy in enhancing individuals’ health in China

[*BMC Public Health*](/)
**volume 25**, Article number: 364 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

3065 Accesses

4 Citations

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8/metrics)

## Abstract

### Background

Health is the cornerstone of individual well-being and a vital factor in socioeconomic development. In an increasingly digitalized world, digital literacy has emerged as one of the indispensable abilities, which not only pertains to an individual’s capacity to acquire, analyze, evaluate, and utilize information but also profoundly influences their health behaviours, health decisions, and overall well-being. This paper uses the 2020 China Family Panel Studies (CFPS) data to explore digital literacy’s impact on individuals’ health.

### Methods

By using the Entropy-TOPSIS method, this paper calculates digital literacy as a three-dimensional construct and adopts Ordered Probit and OLS models to analyze the impact of digital literacy on physical and mental health. Additionally, this paper employs the Two-Stage Least Squares (2SLS) to address endogeneity issues. The indirect effects of income, employment quality, and informal social support on individual’s digital literacy and health nexus are also examined by the mediation effect model.

### Results

The results indicate that digital literacy positively impacts individuals’ physical and mental health, which was confirmed by several robustness tests. The heterogeneity analysis shows that digital literacy is more effective in enhancing the health of middle-aged and older groups as well as rural individuals in China. According to mechanism analysis, digital literacy can enhance individuals’ health by boosting income, improving employment quality, and expanding informal social support; however, the effects vary on physical and mental health.

### Conclusion

Several policy suggestions emanate from the findings. To enhance the positive impact of digital literacy on individuals’ health, the policy makers should emphasize on enhancing digital literacy particularly in rural areas and older age groups, bridging the digital divide, refining employment services and embracing a healthy concept of online social networking.

[Peer Review reports](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8/peer-review)

## Introduction

In the digital era, digital technology has permeated every facet of human life, transforming how we communicate, learn, work and even manage our health. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), digital technology will be an important enabling factor in achieving its triple billion targets [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR1 "World Health Organization. Global strategy on digital health 2020–2025. 2021. 
                  https://iris.who.int/handle/10665/344249
                  
                . Accessed Jan 15, 2024.")].[Footnote 1](#Fn1) People can access health information online, consult with healthcare providers remotely, and monitor their health through wearable devices—all facilitated by digital literacy. This empowerment potentially enhances health outcomes by encouraging informed decision-making and promoting self-care practices. As digital literacy has become an essential skill for navigating the digital world and extracting actionable insights for health, this paper explores the effect of digital literacy on health outcomes in China, which offers a valuable case study in managing health disparities and promoting equitable access to healthcare.[Footnote 2](#Fn2)

A cross-country panel data study based on the WHO Health Equity Assessment Toolkit reveals that internet usage significantly improves health conditions and alleviates health inequality [[2](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR2 "Yu J, Meng S. Impacts of the internet on health inequality and healthcare access: a cross-country study. Front Public Health. 2022;10:935608.")]. Existing literature notes several benefits of internet usage in health. Firstly, internet usage aids individuals in acquiring health knowledge and medical information, enhancing their awareness of health management. Studies in the US find that the open and transparent online doctor-patient diagnostic and treatment processes provide references for other patients with similar symptoms, effectively alleviating information asymmetry between doctors and patients and enabling individuals to better understand their own health status [[3](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR3 "Castleton K, Fong T, Wang-Gillam A, Waqar MA, Jeffe DB, Kehlenbrink L, Gao F, Govindan R. A survey of internet utilization among patients with cancer. Support Care Cancer. 2011;19(8):1183–90.")]. Rock Health’s 2022 Survey shows that 80% of respondents have accessed health care via telemedicine.[Footnote 3](#Fn3) Research indicates that individuals who access health information online exhibit stronger health management awareness and enjoy better health outcomes [[4](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR4 "Dutta-Bergman MJ. Primary sources of health information: comparisons in the domain of health attitudes, health cognitions, and health behaviors. Health Commun. 2004;16(3):273–88.")]. Secondly, the development of online healthcare has overturned the monopoly of doctors in terms of professional information. Individuals now enjoy reduced travel costs and improved convenience in seeking medical advice through online consultations [[5](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR5 "Black AD, Car J, Pagliari C, Anadan C, Cresswell K, Bokun T, McKinstry B, Procter R, Majeed A, Sheikh A. The impact of eHealth on the quality and safety of health care: a systematic overview. PLoS Med. 2011;8(1):e1000387.")]. More importantly, online interventions may be more suitable for high-risk groups such as young adult cancer survivors because of the broad coverage and attractive incentive structures [[6](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR6 "Berg CJ, Stratton E, Giblin J, Esiashvili N, Mertens A. Pilot results of an online intervention targeting health promoting behaviors among young adult cancer survivors. Psycho-oncology. 2014;23(10):1196.")]. Thirdly, the internet is an effective tool for helping people overcome bad habits and prevent and alleviate diseases. The digital multiple health behaviour change intervention in Sweden and the Netherlands has proved effective [[7](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR7 "Bossen D, Bak M, Braam K, Wentink M, Holla J, Visser B, Dallinga J. Online and offline behavior change techniques to promote a healthy lifestyle: a qualitative study. Int J Environ Res Public Health. 2022;19(1): 521."), [8](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR8 "Åsberg K, Blomqvist J, Lundgren O, Henriksson H, Henriksson P, Bendtsen P, Löf M, Bendtsen M. Digital multiple health behaviour change intervention targeting online help seekers: protocol for the COACH randomized factorial trial. BMJ open. 2022;12(7):e061024.")]. The findings within the Chinese context corroborate the literature by underscoring the positive impact of internet usage on health outcomes and elucidate the mediating roles of health knowledge, income, and social participation [[9](#ref-CR9 "Luo X, Pu H, Wang S, Zhong D, Liu F, Li Z. Influence of internet use on Chinese residents’ health: the mediating role of health knowledge. Technol Soc. 2024;76:102413."),[10](#ref-CR10 "Hong YA, Zhou Z, Fang Y, Shi L. The digital divide and health disparities in China: evidence from a national survey and policy implications. J Med Internet Res. 2017;19(9):e317."),[11](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR11 "Hou B, Li Y, Wang H. Internet use and health status among older adults: the mediating role of social participation. Front Public Health. 2022;10: 1072398.")].

However, this digital transformation also presents challenges. There may be a digital health paradox that those high-risk groups, such as individuals with disabilities, are most likely to be excluded from the digital health innovation [[12](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR12 "van Kessel R, Hrzic R, O’Nuallain E, Weir E, Wong BLH, Anderson M, Baron-Cohen S, Mossialos E. Digital health paradox: international policy perspectives to address increased health inequalities for people living with disabilities. J Med Internet Res. 2022;24(2):e33819.")]. Thus, digital health literacy (or eHealth literacy), a set of abilities such as searching for health information online and applying the knowledge gained to solve a health problem [[13](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR13 "Norman CD, Skinner HA. eHEALS: the eHealth literacy scale. J Med Internet Res. 2006;8(4):e507.")], has become increasingly important. On the one hand, as the sheer volume of health-related content online can be overwhelming, and the quality of information varies widely [[14](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR14 "Daraz L, Morrow AS, Ponce OJ, Beuschel B, Farah MH, Katabi A, Alsawas M, Majzoub AM, Benkhadra R, Seisa MO, Ding JF, Prokop L, Murad MH. Can patients trust online health information? A meta-narrative systematic review addressing the quality of health information on the internet. J Gen Intern Med. 2019;34:1884–91.")], individuals with low digital health literacy may not obtain or understand health information correctly, leading to incorrect decisions [[15](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR15 "Manganello J, Gerstner G, Pergolino K, Graham Y, Falisi A, Strogatz D. The relationship of health literacy with use of digital technology for health information: implications for public health practice. J Public Health Manage Pract. 2017;23(4):380–7.")]. In the US context, studies find that digital health literacy positively predicted perceived trust in online health communication channels and sources [[16](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR16 "Paige SR, Krieger JL, Stellefson ML. The influence of eHealth literacy on perceived trust in online health communication channels and sources. J Health Communication. 2017;22(1):53–65.")], and the whole family could benefit from health information sharing from at least one high digital health literacy member [[17](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR17 "Magsamen-Conrad K, Dillon JM, Billotte Verhoff C, Faulkner SL. Online health-information seeking among older populations: family influences and the role of the medical professional. Health Commun. 2019;34(8):859–71.")]. Moreover, digital health literacy is related to self-management of chronic diseases. It is noted that cancer and Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) patients with lower digital health literacy are likely to be less confident about finding information [[18](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR18 "Park H, Moon M, Baeg JH. Association of eHealth literacy with cancer information seeking and prior experience with cancer screening. CIN: Computers Inf Nurs. 2014;32(9):458–63."), [19](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR19 "Stellefson ML, Shuster JJ, Chaney BH, Paige SR, Alber JM, Chaney JD, Sriram PS. Web-based Health Information Seeking and eHealth literacy among patients living with Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD). Health Commun. 2018;33(12):1410–24.")]. On the other hand, individuals with higher digital health literacy are more likely to adopt good health behaviours by seeking health information online. A moderate correlation between digital health literacy and health-related behaviour is observed in a recent meta-analysis [[20](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR20 "Kim K, Shin S, Kim S, Lee E. The relation between eHealth literacy and health-related behaviors: systematic review and meta-analysis. J Med Internet Res. 2023;25: e40778.")]. More specifically, US college students with higher digital health literacy tend to have exercise regimes, sleep, get vaccinations, maintain sexual health, a balanced diet, stable friendships, and a lifestyle free of harmful substances by seeking out related information [[21](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR21 "Britt RK, Collins WB, Wilson K, Linnemeier G, Englebert AM. eHealth literacy and health behaviors affecting modern college students: a pilot study of issues identified by the American College Health Association. J Med Internet Res. 2017;19(12):e392.")]. Similarly, the positive relationship between digital health literacy and dietary behaviours is confirmed in Taiwan [[22](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR22 "Yang SC, Luo YF, Chiang CH. Electronic health literacy and dietary behaviors in Taiwanese college students: cross-sectional study. J Med Internet Res. 2019;21(11):e13140.")] and Germany [[23](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR23 "König L, Kuhlmey A, Suhr R. Digital health literacy of the population in Germany and its association with physical health, mental health, life satisfaction, and health behaviors: nationally representative survey study. JMIR Public Health Surveillance. 2024;10(1):e48685.")]. Digital health literacy can also be related to preventive behaviours. During the COVID period, individuals with higher digital health literacy showed high adherence to wearing surgical masks, washing hands, social distancing, and adding water or bleach to the household drainage system in Hong Kong [[24](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR24 "Guo Z, Zhao SZ, Guo N, Wu Y, Weng X, Wong JY, Lam TH, Wang MP. Socioeconomic disparities in eHealth literacy and preventive behaviors during the COVID-19 pandemic in Hong Kong: cross-sectional study. J Med Internet Res. 2021;23(4):e24577.")]. Thus, digital health literacy may be considered a super determinant of an individual’s health status in a country [[25](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR25 "van Kessel R, Wong BLH, Clemens T, Brand H. Digital health literacy as a super determinant of health: more than simply the sum of its parts. Internet Interventions. 2022;27:100500.")].

Though there has been a proliferation of research on the impacts of digital technology and digital health literacy on individuals’ health, a significant gap exists in understanding the broader implications of digital literacy. Digital literacy is the key factor for individuals to benefit from the advent of digital technology, which is different from digital health literacy [[26](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR26 "Arias López MDP, Ong BA, Borrat Frigola X, Fernández AL, Hicklent RS, Obeles AJT, Rocimo AM, Celi LA. Digital literacy as a new determinant of health: a scoping review. PLOS Digit Health. 2023;2(10):e0000279.")]. More specifically, digital literacy is the set of skills and knowledge that helps individuals live, learn, and work in a digital society, such as accessing, communicating, evaluating, and creating information through digital technologies.[Footnote 4](#Fn4) It encompasses a variety of abilities, including digital general literacy, digital life literacy and digital professional literacy. Although digital literacy provides the foundational skills necessary for engaging with digital health information, it does not necessarily equate to digital health literacy because more specialized competencies are required for effective health decision-making. Moreover, the interaction between digital literacy and other mediating determinants of health, such as socioeconomic and sociocultural factors, needs exploration [[27](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR27 "Crawford A, Serhal E. Digital health equity and COVID-19: the innovation curve cannot reinforce the social gradient of health. J Med Internet Res. 2020;22(6):e19361.")]. Digital literacy not only directly shapes how individuals improve their health status through digital technology, akin to digital health literacy, but also indirectly enhances health levels by affecting factors such as income, employment and social support that contribute to good health. This paper aims to bridge this gap by exploring the relationship between digital literacy and individuals’ health and examining the indirect effects of digital literacy, which have been largely overlooked in the literature.

Our findings contribute to the literature by highlighting the importance of digital literacy in public health and offering a more nuanced view of the mechanisms linking digital literacy to health outcomes. First, while prior studies have predominantly concentrated on the health effects of digital technology and digital health literacy, this paper uniquely delves into the role of digital literacy. It calculates the digital literacy index using the Entropy-TOPSIS method and provides empirical evidence from China that digital literacy positively influences both the physical and mental health of individuals, supporting the notion that digital capabilities are integral to overall well-being. Second, this paper expands the research horizon by identifying income, employment quality, and informal social support as channels through which digital literacy influences health outcomes. This broadens the understanding of the multifaceted factors at play in the digital era that can affect individuals’ health, not only by delving into the direct impacts of digital literacy on individuals’ health but also by meticulously analyzing its indirect influences. Our results suggest that income is a partial mediator between digital literacy and health, which means digital literacy’s economic benefits can lead to improved health. Employment quality also plays a partial mediating role between digital literacy and physical health; however, our two indicators of employment quality (benefits in kind and insurance) completely mediate between digital literacy and mental health. Job satisfaction positively impacts physical and mental health, emphasizing the need for interventions that enhance the job satisfaction of individuals with higher digital literacy. The mediating role of informal social support highlights the social aspects of digital literacy and its potential to strengthen community ties and improve mental health (but not physical health), especially among older people and rural communities. Third, the insights on the heterogeneity of impacts, encompassing variations among age groups and the urban-rural divide, are instrumental in crafting health policies that are more effective and equitable in the digital age. The identification of an adverse effect of digital literacy on the physical health of the youth group and a positive effect for the middle-aged and olds underscores the need for targeted digital literacy interventions that consider life-stage-specific vulnerabilities and resilience. The greater impact in rural areas indicates that targeted provision of digital training should be considered as a public health priority. These findings underscore the importance of a tailored approach to health policy, recognizing that the influence of digital literacy on health outcomes is not uniform across different segments of the population.

The rest of this paper is organized as follows: "[Development of research hypotheses](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#Sec2)" section conducts the theoretical analysis and formulates research hypotheses. "[Data source and methodology](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#Sec3)" section introduces the data source and methodology. "[Result](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#Sec14)" section presents empirical results and "[Discussion](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#Sec23)" section discusses the results. The last section concludes and presents policy recommendations.

## Development of research hypotheses

Our empirical investigation is based on several interrelated theories available in labor economics, health economics, and sociology that deal with human, health, and social capital. As a determinant of health, digital literacy substantially interacts with various intermediate factors; hence, the theoretical underpinning of the nexus is diversified. In this Section, we develop a number of testable hypotheses, taking the lead from those theories.

Firstly, the theory of health economics considers that human capital can improve health [[28](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR28 "Van DE. Health, knowledge and the demand for medical care. Maastricht, the Netherlands: Assen; 1987.")]. Digital literacy is a valuable human capital that can positively impact individuals’ physical and mental health. Individuals possessing heightened levels of digital literacy enjoy enhanced networking and economic backing, improving their quality of life and healthcare conditions, ultimately affecting their physical health [[29](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR29 "McMaughan DJ, Oloruntoba O, Smith ML. Socioeconomic status and access to healthcare: interrelated drivers for healthy aging. Front Public Health. 2020;8:512143.")]. With a high level of digital literacy, they can gain profound insights into disease prevention and healthcare practices through digital media, fostering healthy living habits when the widespread dissemination of health information via online platforms significantly enhances the accessibility of medical resources [[30](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR30 "Wong CK, Yeung DY, Ho HC, Tse KP, Lam CY. Chinese older adults’ internet use for health information. J Appl Gerontol. 2014;33(3):316–35.")]. At the same time, with a higher level of digital literacy, they can effectively alleviate mental stress through the internet. On the one hand, they can use communication devices to keep in touch with their relatives and friends, enabling intimate exchanges through voice chats and video calls even when physical meetings are infrequent [[31](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR31 "Wang MP, Chu JT, Viswanath K, Wan A, Lam TH, Chan SS. Using information and communication technologies for family communication and its association with family well-being in Hong Kong: FAMILY project. J Med Internet Res. 2015;17(8):e207.")]. The care and attention from family members serve as a formidable emotional pillar for these people [[32](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR32 "Francesca Pernice-Duca. Family network support and mental health recovery. J Marital Family Therapy. 2010;36(1):13–27.")]. On the other hand, they can connect with like-minded individuals on online social platforms, increasing their participation in social activities, which is conducive to alleviating negative emotions such as loneliness, anxiety, and depression [[33](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR33 "Asam AE, Samara M, Terry P. Problematic internet use and mental health among British children and adolescents. Addict Behav. 2019;90:428–36.")]. Hence, our study posits the following hypotheses.

**Hypothesis 1a:** Digital literacy has a beneficial impact on individuals’ physical health.

**Hypothesis 1b:** Digital literacy has a beneficial impact on individuals’ mental health.

Secondly, both the human and social capital theories imply that people’s income is impacted by their level of digital literacy. The human capital theory suggests that digital skills provide a human capital advantage, enabling individuals to offer higher productivity and consequently facilitating higher income attainment [[34](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR34 "Tian G, Zhang X. Digital economy, non-agricultural employment, and division of labor. J Manage World. 2022;38(05):72–84 (in Chinese).")]. Additionally, industries such as online streaming, self-media, and e-commerce have emerged as the digital economy thrives, presenting equipped individuals with many job opportunities, ultimately leading to increased income levels [[35](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR35 "Qi Y, Ding S, Liu C. Internet use and labor supply of flexible workers in the context of the digital economy: theory and evidences. Contemp Finance Econ. 2021;5:3–16 (in Chinese).")]. At the same time, the social capital theory posits that mastery of digital technology can fortify an individual’s existing social networks and foster new social connections [[36](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR36 "Tiepoh MGN, Reimer B. Social capital, information flows, and income creation in rural Canada: a cross-community analysis. J Socio-Econ. 2004;33(4):427–48.")]. It addresses the financing needs of individuals embarking on entrepreneurial ventures [[37](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR37 "Shao Y, Sun L. Entrepreneurs’ social capital and venture capital financing. J Bus Res. 2021;136:499–512.")] and enhances the alignment between businesses and job seekers, thereby increasing income [[38](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR38 "Liu B. Peer effects of entrepreneurship: evidence based on CLDS. China Economic Stud. 2020;03:43–58 (in Chinese).")]. Income has two effects on health demand [[39](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR39 "Grossman M. On the concept of health capital and the demand for health. J Polit Econ. 1972;80(2):223–55.")]: (a) the higher income boosts the marginal returns on health investments, raising the demand for health care among individuals; (b) they may opt to purchase medical services as a substitute for time investment in health care to cut healthcare costs because the increase in income signifies an enhancement in individual productivity, boosting the monetary value of time. Hence, our paper proposes the following hypotheses.

**Hypothesis 2a:** Digital literacy positively impacts the physical health of the population through increased income levels.

**Hypothesis 2b:** Digital literacy positively impacts the mental health of the population through increased income levels.

Thirdly, the research in labor economics indicates that digital literacy positively impacts workers’ employability, thereby enhancing the quality of their employment [[40](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR40 "Matli W, Ngoepe M. Capitalizing on digital literacy skills for capacity development of people who are not in education, employment or training in South Africa. Afr J Sci Technol Innov Dev. 2020;12(2):129–39.")]. In the digital age, as work tasks and approaches are undergoing a digital transformation, possessing digital literacy signifies a higher level of human capital, fostering strong competitiveness in the labor market [[41](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR41 "Bejaković P, Mrnjavac Ž. The importance of digital literacy on the labour market. Empl Relations: Int J. 2020;42(4):921–32.")], which can facilitate formal employment and ensure job stability alongside comprehensive social security [[42](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR42 "Leahy D, Dolan D. Digital literacy: A vital competence for 2010? IFIP international conference on key competencies in the knowledge society. Berlin, Heidelberg: Springer Berlin Heidelberg, 2010: 210–221."), [43](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR43 "Kuhn P, Mansour H. Is internet job search still ineffective? Econ J. 2014;124(581):1213–33.")]. At the same time, Grossman’s health economics model suggests that high-quality employment positively influences health [[39](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR39 "Grossman M. On the concept of health capital and the demand for health. J Polit Econ. 1972;80(2):223–55.")]. It is well established that the quality of employment, such as job insecurity, leads to a comparable decline in individuals’ health conditions than unemployment [[44](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR44 "Kim TJ, von dem Knesebeck O. Is an insecure job better for health than having no job at all? A systematic review of studies investigating the health-related risks of both job insecurity and unemployment. BMC Public Health. 2015;15:985.")]. Moreover, the quality of employment, such as working hours, working benefits and insurance, as well as job satisfaction also have an impact on health outcomes. Working hours, especially overtime, are negatively correlated with health outcomes such as body mass index (BMI), cardiovascular disease, diabetes and fatigue, depression, and stress [[45](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR45 "Berniell I, Bietenbeck J. The effect of working hours on health. Econ Hum Biology. 2020;39:100901."), [46](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR46 "Yu J, Leka S. Where is the limit for overtime? Impacts of overtime on employees’ mental health and potential solutions: a qualitative study in China. Front Psychol. 2022;13: 976723.")]. Working benefits and insurance such as benefits in kind and social insurance provided by employers can not only unlock the potential for household consumption, which motivates individuals to invest more in their health but also significantly ease the financial burdens associated with healthcare and retirement to avoid and prevent health risks, thereby improving their health status [[47](#ref-CR47 "O’Campo P, Molnar A, Ng E, Renahy E, Mitchell C, Shankardass K, St John A, Bambra C, Muntaner C. Social welfare matters: a realist review of when, how, and why unemployment insurance impacts poverty and health. Social science and medicine. 2015;132:88–94."),[48](#ref-CR48 "Berkowitz SA, Basu S. Unemployment insurance, health-related social needs, health care access, and mental health during the COVID-19 pandemic. JAMA Intern Med. 2021;181(5):699–702."),[49](/articles/10.1186/s12889-025-21454-8#ref-CR4

# 10 Ways Digital Literacy Curriculum Supports Student Mental Health

![Writer: Connor Cohen]()

Throughout my career as a mental health professional, I have noticed the influence of social media and technology become ever-present in the lives of the students in therapy. It became quite evident that there was a disconnect between how parents and students viewed technology and social media. Parents were far more likely to view new technologies and social media in a negative light. In contrast, their children would often view the same tech and media positively and as a way to connect with their ever-growing digital world.

In an era where technology permeates nearly every aspect of daily life, digital literacy has become an essential skill set for students to navigate the complexities of the modern world. Beyond the practical aspects of using digital tools, a comprehensive digital literacy curriculum can also play a significant role in supporting student mental health and bridging the gap between online safety and the vast opportunities of the online world.

![Children's Mental Health](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/11062b_6db123f3e02e4d1d81bd03d67d9fa742~mv2_d_6000_2950_s_4_2.jpg/v1/fill/w_147,h_72,al_c,q_80,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/11062b_6db123f3e02e4d1d81bd03d67d9fa742~mv2_d_6000_2950_s_4_2.jpg)

Here are 10 ways in which such a curriculum can contribute to the well-being of students:

**Empowering Critical Thinking:** Digital literacy education encourages students to question and evaluate the information they encounter online. By developing critical thinking skills, students become better equipped to discern credible sources from misinformation or harmful content, reducing anxiety stemming from confusion or manipulation.

**Promoting Online Safety:** Learning about internet safety protocols and privacy protection strategies instills a sense of confidence and security in students as they navigate online spaces. Feeling safe and secure online can alleviate stress and anxiety related to cyberbullying, identity theft, or online harassment.

**Fostering Self-Regulation:** Digital literacy curriculum often includes lessons on managing screen time and balancing online activities with offline pursuits. Teaching students to regulate their technology use can prevent feelings of overwhelm or addiction, promoting healthier habits and better overall mental well-being.

**Encouraging Digital Citizenship:** By emphasizing responsible and ethical behavior online, digital literacy education cultivates a sense of belonging and community among students. Feeling like an active participant in digital society can boost self-esteem and reduce feelings of isolation or exclusion.

**Addressing Cyberbullying:** Digital literacy curriculum typically addresses topics such as cyberbullying awareness and prevention strategies. Equipping students with the knowledge and skills to identify and respond to cyberbullying incidents can mitigate the emotional impact and provide a sense of agency in challenging situations.

![Kid on Computer](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/11062b_ec1855f7f8c24df594aac7ff439510df~mv2.jpeg/v1/fill/w_147,h_98,al_c,q_80,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/11062b_ec1855f7f8c24df594aac7ff439510df~mv2.jpeg)

**Enhancing Media Literacy:** Teaching students to critically analyze media messages and representations helps them develop a healthier relationship with digital media. By understanding how media influences perceptions and emotions, students can better navigate media-related stressors and develop resilience to negative media influences.

**Supporting Emotional Expression:** Some digital literacy programs incorporate activities that encourage students to express themselves creatively through digital media. Engaging in digital storytelling or multimedia projects provides an outlet for self-expression, fostering emotional well-being and self-awareness.

**Building Digital Resilience:** Digital literacy education equips students with the skills to adapt and thrive in an ever-changing digital landscape. Teaching resilience in the face of challenges such as cyber threats or information overload empowers students to overcome setbacks and maintain a positive outlook on their digital experiences.

**Cultivating Empathy and Understanding:** Learning about the diverse perspectives and experiences of others online promotes empathy and understanding among students. Developing empathy can strengthen interpersonal relationships and contribute to a more supportive and inclusive online environment, reducing feelings of loneliness or alienation.

**Connecting with Support Resources:** Digital literacy curriculum often includes information on accessing mental health support services and resources online. Educating students about available support networks and encouraging help-seeking behavior can reduce the stigma surrounding mental health issues and provide students with the assistance they need to thrive academically and personally.

A well-designed digital literacy curriculum goes beyond teaching technical skills; it serves as a vital tool for promoting student mental health and well-being in the digital age. One such example is [Cyber Civics](https://www.cybercivics.com/)!

The Cyber Civics curriculum not only empowers students to be ethical digital citizens but also assists students by establishing connections through technology that go beyond screens as it reminds students that people on the other side of the screen have feelings and emotions too. No matter which side of the screen they are on, they are human too! 

By empowering students with knowledge, skills, and support networks, educators can play a crucial role in fostering a generation of digitally literate and emotionally resilient individuals.

## Abstract

### Introduction

Harnessing new digital technologies can improve access to health care but can also widen the health divide for those with poor digital literacy. This scoping review aims to assess the current situation of low digital health literacy in terms of its definition, reach, impact on health and interventions for its mitigation.

### Methods

A comprehensive literature search strategy was composed by a qualified medical librarian. Literature databases [Medline (Ovid), Embase (Ovid), Scopus, and Google Scholar] were queried using appropriate natural language and controlled vocabulary terms along with hand-searching and citation chaining. We focused on recent and highly cited references published in English. Reviews were excluded. This scoping review was conducted following the methodological framework of Arksey and O’Malley.

### Results

A total of 268 articles were identified (263 from the initial search and 5 more added from the references of the original papers), 53 of which were finally selected for full text analysis. Digital health literacy is the most frequently used descriptor to refer to the ability to find and use health information with the goal of addressing or solving a health problem using technology. The most utilized tool to assess digital health literacy is the eHealth literacy scale (eHEALS), a self-reported measurement tool that evaluates six core dimensions and is available in various languages. Individuals with higher digital health literacy scores have better self-management and participation in their own medical decisions, mental and psychological state and quality of life. Effective interventions addressing poor digital health literacy included education/training and social support.

### Conclusions

Although there is interest in the study and impact of poor digital health literacy, there is still a long way to go to improve measurement tools and find effective interventions to reduce the digital health divide.

## Author summary

This scoping review aimed to investigate the impact of low digital literacy (DL) on health as well as DL and digital health definitions, instruments used to assess it and interventions useful for its mitigation. We performed a comprehensive literature search strategy which identified 53 articles for analysis. Digital health literacy was the most commonly used term to describe the ability to find and use health information using technology. The eHealth literacy scale (eHEALS) was found to be the most utilized tool to assess digital health literacy. Higher scores on the eHEALS were linked to better self-management and participation in medical decisions, improved mental health, and overall quality of life. Overall, this study highlights the need to address the digital health divide and improve access to digital health information and tools for those with low DL. This can be achieved by developing effective interventions to improve digital health literacy, like education/training and social support and increasing awareness of the importance of digital health literacy for better health outcomes. The findings of this study can inform policymakers and healthcare providers on the need to address the issue of low digital health literacy to improve health equity and outcomes.

## Introduction

Digital technologies are transforming health, health care, and public health systems across the world, and they have a great potential to improve population and individual´s health and wellbeing [[1](#pdig.0000279.ref001)]. Technology has advanced in such a way that it has made it possible to expand the type of interactions available between the user and the medical provider or the healthcare systems. Obtaining health information and requesting appointments online, virtual visits, asynchronous digital messaging with healthcare professionals, health tracking wearables and self-monitoring devices, are all technologies available nowadays. These systems bring many advantages; they make it possible to scale information processing, administrative processes and facilitate access to healthcare through virtual visits. Using a video or an online appointment app enables providers to serve hundreds of people that can be attended simultaneously and travel can be avoided for in-person appointments that do not require a physical examination or tests [[2](#pdig.0000279.ref002)]. However, weak governance of digital transformations can also lead to uneven effects globally, increasing health inequities. This reflects the paradox of digital health that we are currently facing: the potential that digital health innovations hold can be transformational for delivering care to underserved population groups (rural areas, aging patients, minorities, or persons with disabilities) but these groups are most likely to be excluded from the digital world through their sociodemographic characteristics [[3](#pdig.0000279.ref003),[4](#pdig.0000279.ref004)].

The digital transformation, which a priori has many advantages, may contribute to further increasing the inequalities that already exist in access to healthcare thus generating a “digital divide”. “Digital divide” is a term used to encompass a wide range of social differences in access to and use of digital equipment and services, especially personal computers and smartphones, and the ability to access the Internet, both in terms of physical connection and ease of use. As health care becomes more reliant on technology-based tools, the digital divide stands to further exacerbate existing health care access disparities [[2](#pdig.0000279.ref002)].

Digital technologies should be recognized nowadays as a key determinant of health, similar to socioeconomic status, income, education, age, race, ethnicity and gender [[4](#pdig.0000279.ref004)]. Although almost the entire world population now lives within reach of some form of mobile broadband or internet service and mobile phones are becoming ubiquitous, only half of people worldwide use the internet and have basic information and communications technology skills [[5](#pdig.0000279.ref005)]. This gap between internet access and use shows that there are multiple barriers to meaningful access that need to be addressed, especially lack of science, technology, engineering, and mathematics education, digital skills and digital literacy.

Digital literacy can be defined as the varying ability of both children and adults to use digital technologies and understand their risks. It refers not only to the applied technical skills necessary to use and access the internet, but also to the capacity to critically and confidently engage with the online environment. More broadly, as a determinant of health, it has been emphasized that digital literacy substantially interacts with other intermediate health factors and social determinants, to influence both access to digital health resources and wider health equity outcomes [[6](#pdig.0000279.ref006)]. Health literacy–the ability to obtain, read, understand and use health-care information to make appropriate/ informed health decisions [[7](#pdig.0000279.ref007)]–is increasingly becoming a core skill for health-related information on the Internet. Digital health literacy, at first glance, can be regarded as the convergence of digital literacy and health literacy. However, the reality is likely more complex because each competence domain of digital and health literacy may affect one or more competence domains of digital health literacy [[3](#pdig.0000279.ref003)]. On the other side, different terms are used interchangeably in the literature to refer to digital health literacy. According to the National Institutes of Health All of Us Research Program, digital health literacy is “the ability to seek, find, understand, and appraise health information from electronic sources and apply the knowledge gained to addressing or solving a health problem” [[8](#pdig.0000279.ref008)]. Other terms are used depending on the source(s) of the health information. While mHealth literacy focuses on information gathered with the use of mobile devices, eHealth literacy focuses on information gathered from online resources and telehealth literacy specifically focuses on telehealth platforms [[1](#pdig.0000279.ref001)]. In any of these cases, low digital health literacy can carry with it several consequences. Primarily, it can deepen health inequities in an increasingly digitized healthcare landscape. Patients who do not know how to use digital health tools, don’t see the importance of those tools, or can’t access them in their preferred language, ultimately won’t use them. And that puts them at a disadvantage for patient engagement and health improvement. Digital health literacy has recently been acknowledged as one of the “super social determinants of health” because it has implications for the wider social determinants of health [[3](#pdig.0000279.ref003),[6](#pdig.0000279.ref006),[9](#pdig.0000279.ref009)] In order to design effective strategies to address this new health determinant, it is necessary to recognise its consequences and the populations it affects. We therefore decided to carry out this scoping review that seek to determine the effects of poor digital literacy on health, specifically looking to define poor digital health literacy, identify populations at risk, the health outcomes affected, its consequences, and interventions targeted to reduce the digital health literacy gap.

## Methods

This scoping review was conducted following the methodological framework of Arksey and O’Malley [[10](#pdig.0000279.ref010)]. The review process was structured according to the following stages:

### Identification of the research question

The research question was stated as, “How does digital health literacy affect health?”. Specific objectives include (1) to define digital health literacy, identify existing assessment tools and groups potentially affected by poor digital health literacy (2) to identify the health outcomes affected by poor digital health literacy and its consequences, and (3) to identify interventions targeted to reduce the digital health literacy gap.

### Identification of relevant studies

On May 11, 2022, a comprehensive search of the literature was constructed and performed by a qualified medical librarian. Medline (Ovid), Embase (Ovid), Scopus, and Google Scholar were queried using both natural language and controlled vocabulary terms for telehealth, digital health, digital literacy, computer proficiency, vulnerable populations, health outcomes, etc. We focused on recent (last 5 years) and highly cited references published in English. Conference abstracts were excluded. The search strategy is detailed in [S1 Text](#pdig.0000279.s002). Reference lists of the studies found through database searches were also checked, especially for the systematic reviews and scoping reviews.

### Selection of studies

Original articles on digital literacy and digital health literacy were included if these answered any of the study objectives. Papers that focused on technology use and access instead of digital literacy were excluded. Articles on interventions targeted to reduce the digital literacy gap were excluded if the intervention was not tested in a study population. The initial search returned a large number of results, including non-original articles (systematic reviews and scoping reviews), editorials, letters, commentaries, study protocols, and other publications not deemed relevant that were therefore excluded. Articles without available full text and papers that were not available in English were also excluded. Five authors (MA, BO, AL, XB, AO and AR) independently screened titles and abstracts identified by the electronic search and applied the selection criteria to potentially relevant papers. Any disagreements were resolved by consensus within the group. Data from the selected relevant papers were extracted by one author using a standardised checklist and checked by a second ([S1 Template](#pdig.0000279.s003)).

### Charting of data

The following key items were obtained based on our consensus as to what information should be collected from the individual studies: author of the article; journal and year the article was published in; article type and objective; setting (country), study population, and sample size; definition and assessment of digital literacy or digital health literacy; health outcomes affected by digital health literacy and its consequences; and interventions targeted to reduce the digital health literacy gap.

The following data were sought for studies that defined digital literacy, digital health literacy, and its related concepts: concept(s) or term(s) used; definition; theoretical framework or model. For studies that employed assessment tools for digital health literacy, the following data were sought: assessment tool; author; elements considered by the assessment tool; aim or intended use of the assessment tool; mode (self-rated versus performance-based); scoring; language; and reliability, Cronbach α. For studies that tackled the correlation between digital health literacy and health outcomes, the following data were sought: health outcomes assessed; and main conclusion on the association between digital health literacy and health outcomes. For studies on interventions targeted to improve digital health literacy, the following data were sought: intervention category; intervention; author; setting; and study population.

### Reporting of results

Pertinent data from the included studies were summarized and analyzed in a narrative, and presented in groups or themes wherever applicable. A critique of the methods and outcomes of the included studies is beyond the scope of this review.

This review was not registered and neither a protocol was prepared. All data are in the manuscript and/or supporting information files. The data used to construct Figs [1](#pdig-0000279-g001) and [2](#pdig-0000279-g002) are reported in table format in [S1 Data](#pdig.0000279.s004).

## Results

A total of 268 articles were identified in the study. Of these, 263 came from the electronic search and five came from the references of the literature and systematic reviews identified from the electronic search. [Fig 1](#pdig-0000279-g001) lists the number of studies included and excluded per step according to the diagram adapted from the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses: The PRISMA Statement [[11](#pdig.0000279.ref011)].

Four duplicates were identified. Of the remaining articles, 199 did not proceed beyond the screening stage due to any of the following: no abstract; out of scope; non-original data, meta-analysis, or reviews; editorials, letters, or commentaries; or protocol. Twelve articles were further excluded due to unavailable full text.

### Characteristics of included studies

The main characteristics of the included studies are described in [S1 Table](#pdig.0000279.s005).

The 53 studies that reached analysis were published in a diverse range of journals. Among the included studies, the oldest was published in 2016 [[12](#pdig.0000279.ref012)] while the most recent ones were published in April 2022 [[13](#pdig.0000279.ref013), [14](#pdig.0000279.ref014)]. The majority of the studies were published in 2021 (19/53; 35.85%) followed by 2020 (15/53; 28.30%).

Of the 53 included studies, 44 were cross-sectional studies (44/53; 83.01%). Only six (6/53; 11.32%) studies were longitudinal: three of them experimental [[14](#pdig.0000279.ref014)–[16](#pdig.0000279.ref016)], the rest pre-post observational studies [[17](#pdig.0000279.ref017)–[19](#pdig.0000279.ref019)]. The last three studies were mixed methodology studies [[20](#pdig.0000279.ref020)–[22](#pdig.0000279.ref022)].

Most studies (20/53; 37.74%) were conducted in North America (17 of them in the United States of America, 2 in Canada, and 1 in Mexico), 17 in Asia (6 in China, 6 in Korea, 1 in Pakistan, 1 in Taiwan, 1 in Vietnam, and 1 in multiple countries), 9 in Europe, 2 in Australia and 1 in Africa ([Fig 2](#pdig-0000279-g002)).

### Definition of digital health literacy

Different terms were used across the studies to refer to digital health literacy, including electronic health literacy, eHealth literacy, mHealth literacy, telehealth literacy, and mobile health proficiency. Among these, the most frequently used in the included studies is eHealth literacy (30/53; 56.60%) followed by digital health literacy (12/53; 22.64%). These concepts similarly refer to the ability to find and use health information with the goal of addressing or solving a health problem using technology. However, these are differentiated by the source(s) of the health information [[23](#pdig.0000279.ref023)–[26](#pdig.0000279.ref026)]. mHealth literacy focuses on information gathered with the use of mobile devices [[25](#pdig.0000279.ref025)] while eHealth literacy focuses on information gathered from online resources [[23](#pdig.0000279.ref023)]. Telehealth literacy specifically focuses on telehealth platforms [[27](#pdig.0000279.ref027)].

In the included studies, digital health literacy was most often used interchangeably with the earlier term eHealth literacy [[23](#pdig.0000279.ref023)]. Whereas eHealth literacy is limited to information from Web 1.0 platforms viewed by users in a passive manner, digital health literacy incorporates information from Web 2.0 platforms with interactive content including social media, blogs, and video sharing sites [[23](#pdig.0000279.ref023)–[25](#pdig.0000279.ref025)]. Digital health literacy is therefore a broader concept compared to eHealth literacy.

A more specific concept related to digital health literacy encountered in one included study is digital healthy diet literacy, defined as the ability to access and appraise digital healthy-diet-related information to improve healthy eating behavior and health outcomes [[28](#pdig.0000279.ref028)].

The term digital literacy instead of digital health literacy was used in seven of the included studies (7/53; 13.21%). Compared to digital health literacy and eHealth literacy, digital literacy is a broader term as it refers to the ability to find and apply digital information. Other terms used by the included studies for the same concept include digital competency [[29](#pdig.0000279.ref029)], digital capability level [[30](#pdig.0000279.ref030)], mobile phone digital literacy [[31](#pdig.0000279.ref031)], new media literacy and technological literacy [[32](#pdig.0000279.ref032)].

The conceptual framework of eHealth literacy by Norman and Skinner [[23](#pdig.0000279.ref023)] was employed by the majority of studies (32/53; 60.37%). Four studies [[33](#pdig.0000279.ref033)–[36](#pdig.0000279.ref036)] employed the e-health literacy framework by Norgaard et al. [[24](#pdig.0000279.ref024)] and one study [[37](#pdig.0000279.ref037)] employed the transactional model of ehealth literacy by Paige et al. [[38](#pdig.0000279.ref038)]. In Norman and Skinner’s framework, eHealth literacy is likened to the pistil that holds the petals of the lily flower together, similar to how eHealth literacy ties together six core skills: traditional literacy, health literacy, information literacy, scientific literacy, media literacy, and computer literacy [[23](#pdig.0000279.ref023)]. In contrast, the framework by Norgaard et al. [[24](#pdig.0000279.ref024)] encompasses domains largely dependent on the individual (domain 1: ability to process information; domain 2: engagement in own health), domains largely dependent on the system (domain 6: access to digital services that work; domain 7: digital services that suit individual needs), and domains on the dynamics between the individual and system (domain 3: ability to actively engage with digital services; domain 4: feel safe and in control; domain 5: motivated to engage with digital services). The transactional model of eHealth literacy, on the other hand, highlights the transactional features central to eHealth literacy and outlines four operational skills: functional, communicative, critical and translational [[38](#pdig.0000279.ref038)].

In [S2 Table](#pdig.0000279.s006), we detail the concepts related to digital health literacy and its corresponding theoretical framework employed in the included studies.

### Measurement of digital health literacy

Twenty different assessment tools were employed by the included studies to assess digital literacy and/or digital health literacy ([Table 1](#pdig-0000279-t001)).

![thumbnail](article/figure/image?size=inline&id=10.1371/journal.pdig.0000279.t001)

<https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pdig.0000279.t001>

Forty-five of the included studies (45/53; 84.90%) used at least one of these assessment tools. ([S3 Table](#pdig.0000279.s007)) The tools differ in terms of evaluation elements, applicable groups, and intended use. Six of these specifically focus on digital literacy [[29](#pdig.0000279.ref029),[30](#pdig.0000279.ref030),[39](#pdig.0000279.ref039)–[42](#pdig.0000279.ref042)], six on eHealth literacy [[12](#pdig.0000279.ref012),[33](#pdig.0000279.ref033),[36](#pdig.0000279.ref036),[37](#pdig.0000279.ref037),[43](#pdig.0000279.ref043),[44](#pdig.0000279.ref044)], four on digital health literacy [[45](#pdig.0000279.ref045)–[48](#pdig.0000279.ref048)], one on digital healthy diet literacy [[49](#pdig.0000279.ref049)], one on telehealth literacy [[27](#pdig.0000279.ref027)], one on health information literacy [[50](#pdig.0000279.ref050)] and one on a combination of eHealth and mHealth literacy [[51](#pdig.0000279.ref051)]. Most of these tools are self-rated Likert scales except for eHealth Literacy Assessment [eHLA) toolkit [[36](#pdig.0000279.ref036)] and Digital Health Literacy Instrument [DHLI] [[45](#pdig.0000279.ref045)] which both employ a combination of self-rated and performance-based assessment, and Digital Literacy Evaluation [DILE) tool [[41](#pdig.0000279.ref041)], which is performance-based.

The eHealth literacy scale [eHEALS) by Norman and Skinner [[43](#pdig.0000279.ref043)] is the most widely used in the included studies (24/53; 45.28%). Five studies [[13](#pdig.0000279.ref013),[22](#pdig.0000279.ref022),[52](#pdig.0000279.ref052)–[54](#pdig.0000279.ref054)] used the DHLI [[45](#pdig.0000279.ref045)], and two studies [[49](#pdig.0000279.ref049),[55](#pdig.0000279.ref055)] used the Digital Healthy Diet Literacy [DDL) tool [[49](#pdig.0000279.ref049)].

Twelve of the twenty assessment tools identified are available in English, five in Chinese, three in Danish, four in Korean, two in Vietnamese, and two in Spanish. Four of the assessment tools are available in several languages. The included studies used different versions of eHEALS (English [[15](#pdig.0000279.ref015),[18](#pdig.0000279.ref018),[28](#pdig.0000279.ref028),[56](#pdig.0000279.

enriched_points.point_title: Importance of Digital Literacy in Mental Health
enriched_points.expanded_content: Digital literacy enhances individuals' ability to access health information, make informed decisions, and engage in self-care, positively impacting mental health outcomes.
Providing training programs equips individuals with essential digital skills, enabling them to navigate online resources safely and effectively, thus promoting better mental health management.
Access to online courses and tutorials can empower individuals to enhance their digital literacy, fostering confidence in using technology for health-related needs.
Community initiatives focusing on digital literacy offer social support and networking opportunities, helping individuals build connections that can alleviate feelings of isolation.
By improving digital literacy, individuals gain the confidence to seek mental health resources online, enabling timely access to support and reducing stigma surrounding mental health issues.
